text: The surgeon opens over the sacral promontory and performs a posterior and right lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. The dissection navigates through scarring and redundancy in the retroperitoneum, revealing the crackly fascia and smooth glistening tissue.

The surgeon adjusts the robotic setup by port hopping the camera from position three to two, using two right ports and one left port, with scissors positioned in port three to optimize the angle for pelvic dissection.

The surgeon continues the dissection down posteriorly and right laterally, carefully preserving the right lateral stalks. The presacral veins are visible, and the surgeon maintains hemostasis while adhering to the proper anatomical planes to avoid vascular injury.
The camera is adjusted to provide a view of the deep pelvis, where the surgeon lifts the mesorectum off the fascia of the levator muscles using robotic assistance.
The surgeon maneuvers the robotic instruments anteriorly, revealing the white tissue of the vagina as they locate the rectovaginal septum in a patient who has previously undergone a hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses digital palpation from below to confirm the proper dissection plane and assess the depth of the dissection. The surgeon identifies the need to continue dissecting lower while being mindful of vessels in the area.
The surgeon continues dissection in the posterior plane, identifying the anal coccygeal ligament. The surgical field is prepared for the next step, which involves performing a pexy.

The surgeon meticulously cleans the sacral promontory, removing nerves and vessels from the bone. They take additional bites while dissecting the mesorectum, using robotic arms to maintain tension on the bowel and tie knots. A suture breaks, but the surgeon retains enough length to complete the knot tying.
The surgeon uses silk sutures to perform a unilateral pexy, taking two bites to secure the rectosigmoid colon without causing a kink at the attachment site.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal opening along the anterior and right lateral mesorectum using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, aiming to prevent the small bowel from descending into the posteriorly dissected space.
The surgeon inspects the pexy site, ensuring appropriate tension and checking for any serosal injuries on the bowel, marking the conclusion of the operation.
